blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XVI
THE MILNOR FORMULAby P. DELIGNE

In this exposé, we transpose to characteristic \(p\) the formula given by Milnor [3] (see also [2]), which in certain cases makes it possible to calculate dimensions of spaces of vanishing cycles. The proof is global in nature, and only allows us to treat the "geometric" case of a base which is a henselian trait of equal characteristic with perfect residue field.
1. Statement of the problem

1.1. Let \(S\) be a regular scheme purely of dimension one, \(s\) a closed point of \(S\) with perfect residue field, \(f:X\to S\) a flat morphism of finite type, \(x_o\) a closed point of the fiber \(X_s\) and \(\bar x_o\) a geometric point of \(X\) localized at \(x_o\). We suppose that
\(a\) \(x_o\) is an isolated point of non-smoothness of \(f\): there exist \(n\) and a neighborhood \(U\) of \(x_o\), purely of relative dimension \(n\), such that \(U-\{x_o\}\) is smooth over \(S\);
\(b\) \(X\) is regular at \(x_o\).
Under these hypotheses, \(X\) is a relative complete intersection over \(S\) at \(x_o\). We refer to exposé VI. 3.10 for the definition of \(D^1_{X/S}\). In the neighborhood \(U\) of \(x_o\), we have
\[ D^1_{U/S}=H^1(R\operatorname{Hom}(L_{U/S},\mathcal O_U)) =\operatorname{Ext}^1(\Omega^1_{U/S},\mathcal O_U)\ ; \]
in this neighborhood, \(D^1_{X/S}\) is of finite length and of support contained in \(\{x_o\}\).
Definition 1.2. The Milnor number \(\mu=\mu(X/S,\bar x_o)\) of \(X/S\) at \(\bar x_o\) is the length of the \(\mathcal O_{X,x_o}\)-module defined by \(D^1_{X/S}\).

Example 1.3. Let us take for \(S\) a smooth curve over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Replacing \(X\) by a neighborhood of \(x_o\), we may assume \(X\) smooth over \(k\). The cotangent complex then reduces to the following resolution of \(\Omega^1_{X/S}\)
\[ L_{X/S}=[f^*\Omega^1_{S/k}\longrightarrow\Omega^1_{X/k}]. \]

Let us choose a local coordinate \(t:S\to\mathbb E^1_k\) on \(S\) at \(s\) (\(t\) étale at \(s\)), let us again denote by \(f\) the composite \(t\circ f\), and let \((x_i)_{1\le i\le n+1}\) be a system of local coordinates on \(X\) at \(x_o\). At this point, at \(x_o\), \(f^*\Omega^1_{S/k}\) has basis \(df\), and \(\Omega^1_{X/k}\) has as basis the \(dx_i\). From then on, \(D^1_{X/S}\) is identified with the quotient of \(\mathcal O_X\) by the ideal generated by the \(\partial f/\partial x_i\)
\[ \tag{1.3.1}\mu=\dim_k\bigl(\mathcal O_{X,x_o}/(\tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1},\ldots, \tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_{n+1}})\bigr). \]
This is the definition which appears in Milnor.

Example 1.4. Suppose that \(X\) is the subscheme of \(\mathbb E^{n+1}_S\) defined by an equation \(g\). Let \(i:X\to\mathbb E^{n+1}_S\) be the inclusion morphism. The cotangent complex then reduces to
\[ [(g)/(g)^2\longrightarrow i^*\Omega^1_{\mathbb E^{n+1}_S/S}] \]

and \(D^1_{X/S}\) is isomorphic to the quotient of \(\mathcal O_X\) by the ideal generated by the \((\partial g/\partial x_i)_{1\le i\le n+1}\), or again to the quotient of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb E^{n+1}_S}\) by the ideal generated by \(g\) and the \(\partial g/\partial x_i\).

Example 1.5. Let us take for \(S\) the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring \(V\) with perfect residue field and field of fractions \(K\). Let \(X\) be the normalization of \(S\) in a separable extension \(K'\) of \(K\). By 1.4 and [5] III 7.14 pg 68, \(D^1_{X/S}\) is then isomorphic to the quotient of \(\mathcal O_X\) by the different \(\mathfrak D_{X/S}\). If \(\bar x_o\) is a geometric point of \(X_s\), localized at \(x_o\in X_s\), the Milnor number at \(\bar x_o\) is thus
\[ \tag{1.5.1}\mu=\ell g(\mathcal O_{X,x_o}/\mathfrak D_{X/S}). \]

Let \(\bar s\) be a geometric point of \(S\) localized at the closed point \(s\) of \(S\), and let \(d_{K'/K}\) be an element of \(V\) which generates the discriminant ideal, of valuation \(v(d_{K'/K})\). It follows from 1.5.1 that

1.6. Let us keep the hypotheses of 1.1, denote by \(\bar s\) the geometric point of \(S\) which is the image of \(\bar x_o\), let \(S_{(\bar s)}\) be the spectrum of the strict henselization of \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\) at \(\bar s\), and let \(X_{(\bar s)}\) be the inverse image of \(X\) over \(S_{(\bar s)}\). Let \(\bar\eta\) be the spectrum of an algebraic closure of the field of fractions \(k(\eta)\) of \(S_{(\bar s)}\), and \(I=\operatorname{Gal}(k(\bar\eta)/k(\eta))\) the inertia group.

Let \(\ell\) be a prime number distinct from the residue characteristic of \(S\). In a neighborhood of the point \(\bar x_o\) of \(X_{\bar s}\), the sheaves of vanishing cycles \(\Phi^i_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)=R^i\Phi_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)\) on \(X_{\bar s}\), relative to the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z/\ell\) on \(X_{(\bar s)}\), are concentrated at \(\bar x_o\); moreover, the inertia group \(I\) acts on the \(\mathbb Z/\ell\)-vector spaces \(\Phi^i_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar x_o}\).
These are finite-dimensional (XIII 2.4.2).

1.7. Let \(I_1\) be a finite quotient of the inertia group. Let us recall that the Swan character \(\operatorname{Sw}\) of \(I_1\) is the difference \(a-u\) of the Artin character \(a\) and the character of the augmentation representation \(u\). Let us also recall that there exists, up to isomorphism, a single projective \(\mathbb Z_\ell[I_1]\)-module \(S(I_1)\) with character \(\operatorname{Sw}\) (see [6]).

Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional vector space over \(\mathbb Z/\ell\), equipped with an action of \(I\): \(\rho:I\to\operatorname{GL}(V)\). Suppose that \(\rho\) factors through \(I_1\). The wild dimension of \(V\) is then
\[ \tag{1.7.1}\dim\operatorname{Sw}(V)=\dim_{\mathbb Z/\ell}\operatorname{Hom}_{I_1} (S(I_1)\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Z/\ell,V). \]
This integer is independent of the choice of \(I_1\), for if \(H\) is normal in \(I_1\), we have \(S(I_1/H)\sim S(I_1)_H\).
We further set
\[ \dim\operatorname{tot}(V)=\dim\operatorname{Sw}(V)+\dim_{\mathbb Z/\ell}(V) \]
and we call this number the total dimension of \(V\).

Definition 1.8. With the terminology of 1.6 and 1.7, we call the number of vanishing cycles of \(X/S\) at \(\bar x_o\) (resp. the wild number of vanishing cycles, resp. the total number of vanishing cycles) the integer

\[ (-1)^n\dim\Phi_{\bar x_o}\ \mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm dfn}}\ (-1)^n\sum_i(-1)^i\dim\Phi^i_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar x_o}, \] \[ (\text{resp. }(-1)^n\dim\operatorname{Sw}\Phi_{\bar x_o}\ \mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm dfn}}\ (-1)^n\sum_i(-1)^i\dim\operatorname{Sw}\Phi^i_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar x_o}, \] \[ \text{resp. }(-1)^n\dim\operatorname{tot}\Phi_{\bar x_o}\ \mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm dfn}}\ (-1)^n\sum_i(-1)^i\dim\operatorname{tot}\Phi^i_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar x_o} =(-1)^n(\dim\Phi_{\bar x_o}+\dim\operatorname{Sw}\Phi_{\bar x_o})). \]
Conjecture 1.9. Under the hypotheses of 1.1, the Milnor number \(\mu\) of \(X/S\) at \(\bar x_o\) is equal to the total number of vanishing cycles of \(X/S\) at \(\bar x_o\).

Remark 1.10. Let \(S\) be a henselian trait with imperfect residue field and \(f:X\to S\) a morphism smooth except at \(x_o\in X_s\). We suppose \(X\) regular at \(x_o\). The definition 1.2 of the Milnor number \(\mu\) still makes sense under these hypotheses, but I no longer have a geometric interpretation of \(\mu\) to propose.

1.11. In the rest of this section, we suppose that \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\) is a henselian trait with algebraically closed residue field, with closed point \(s\), that \(f:X\to S\) is a flat morphism of finite type purely of relative dimension \(n\), that \(X\) is regular, that \(x_o\) is a closed point of \(X_s\) and that \(X-\{x_o\}\) is smooth over \(S\).
This does not restrict the generality.

blocks.title: Proposition 1.12.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.12.

If \(n=0\) (i.e. \(X/S\) finite), conjecture 1.9 is true for \((X/S,x_o)\).
We may assume that \(X\) is the normalization of \(S\) in a finite extension \(K'\) of the field of fractions \(K\). By 1.5, we have \(\mu=v(d_{K'/K})\).

The extension \(K'\) is defined by an open subgroup \(H\) of the inertia group \(I\). Let \(H'\subset H\) be an open normal subgroup of \(I\) and \(I'=I/H'\). Let \(s_{I/H}\) be the character of the permutation representation of \(I'\) on \(I/H\), \(a_{I'}\) the character of the Artin representation, \(\operatorname{Sw}_{I'}\) that of the Swan representation and \(u_{I'}\) that of the augmentation representation. By Artin's “Führerdiss- kriminantenproduktformel”, the discriminant ideal is the Artin conductor of \(s_{I/H}\) ([5] VI 3 cor 1 pg 111):
We have

\[ a_{I'}=u_{I'}+\operatorname{Sw}_{I'}, \] \[ (u_{I'},s_{I/H})=\# I/H-1 \] \[ (\operatorname{Sw}_{I'},s_{I/H})= \dim_{\mathbb Z/\ell}\operatorname{Hom}_{I'}(S(I')\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Z/\ell, \mathbb Z/\ell^{I/H}) \] \[ =\dim_{\mathbb Z/\ell}\operatorname{Hom}_{I'}(S(I')\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Z/\ell, \operatorname{Coker}(\mathbb Z/\ell\to\mathbb Z/\ell^{I/H})) \]
and thus
\[ \mu=(\# I/H-1)+\dim\operatorname{Sw}(\operatorname{Coker}(\mathbb Z/\ell\to\mathbb Z/\ell^{I/H})). \]
One verifies immediately that \(\Phi^i_{\bar x_o}=0\) for \(i\ne0\) and that
\[ \Phi^0_{\bar x_o}=\operatorname{Coker}(\mathbb Z/\ell\to\mathbb Z/\ell^{I/H})\ ; \]
the formula follows.

blocks.title: Proposition 1.13.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.13.

If \(X_s\) presents at \(x_o\) an ordinary quadratic singularity, conjecture 1.9 is true for \((X/S,x_o)\).
The problem is local for the étale topology on \(X\). We may thus assume that \(X_s\) is the subscheme of \(\mathbb E^{n+1}_S\) defined by an equation \(f\):

\(a\) being in the maximal ideal of \(V\), and that \(x_o\) is the origin of coordinates. Moreover, for \(X\) to be regular at \(x_o\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(a\) be a uniformizer. By 1.4, we have
\[ \mu=\ell g(\mathcal O_{\mathbb E_S^{2m}}/(f,\tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}))=1. \]

By [1], \(\Phi^i_{\bar x_o}=0\) for \(i\ne n\), and \(\Phi^n_{\bar x_o}\) is of dimension one, with trivial action of the inertia. The wild number of vanishing cycles is thus zero, and the total number of vanishing cycles is 1, which verifies 1.9.
Case 2. \(n=\dim(X_s)=2m\), \(\operatorname{car}(k(s))\ne2\).
Under these hypotheses, we may take \(X\subset\mathbb E_S^{n+1}\) defined by an equation \(f\)

\(a\) being a uniformizer. Here again, \(\mu=1\), \(\Phi^i_{\bar x_o}=0\) for \(i\ne n\) and \(\Phi^n_{\bar x_o}\) is of dimension one. This time the inertia group acts through \(\pm1\) on \(\Phi^n_{\bar x_o}\), hence tamely. The wild number of vanishing cycles is again zero, and 1.9 is verified.
Case 3. \(n=\dim(X_s)=2m\), \(\operatorname{car}(k(s))=2\).
This time, we may take \(X\subset\mathbb E_S^{n+1}\) defined by the equation \(f\):

with \(a\) and \(b\) in the maximal ideal. For \(X\) to be regular, it is necessary and sufficient that \(b\) be a uniformizer; for the generic fiber of \(X\) to be smooth, it is necessary and sufficient that \(a^2-4b\ne0\).
The Milnor number \(\mu\) of \(X/S\) coincides with that of \(X_o\subset\mathbb E_S^1\) defined by the equation \(f_o\)

Similarly, the \(\Phi^i_{\bar x_o}\) are zero for \(i\ne n\), \(\Phi^n_{\bar x_o}\) is of dimension one, \(I\) acts on \(\Phi^n_{\bar x_o}\) through \(\pm1\) and the subgroup of index two of \(I\) which acts trivially corre- sponds to the extension of \(K\) with equation (1). The total number of vanishing cycles of \(X/S\) at \(x_o\) thus coincides with that relative to \(X_o/S\), and the validity of 1.9 in this case follows from 1.12. The Milnor number is given by (1.5)
2. The “geometric” case

We propose to show that conjecture 1.9 is true in equal charac- teristic. The key geometric result is the following. The possibility of using it was suggested to me by conversations with B. Saint-Donat.

blocks.title: Proposition 2.1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1.

Let \(S\) be a smooth connected projective curve over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(X\) a proper smooth variety over \(k\), purely of di- mension \(n+1\), and \(f:X\to S\) a morphism smooth outside a finite set \(F\) of points of \(X\). The Euler-Poincaré characteristics (in étale cohomology) of \(S\), \(X\) and of the geometric generic fiber \(X_{\bar\eta}\) of \(f\) then satisfy

\[ \tag{2.1.1}\chi(X)=\chi(S)\chi(X_{\bar\eta})-(-1)^n\sum_{x\in F}\mu(X/S,x). \]
We know that the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(X\) coincides with the trace (or integral over \(X\)) of the last Chern class of the tangent bundle
Let us set
\[ \Omega_X^{\prime 1}=(\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1})\oplus f^*\Omega_S^1. \]
The total Chern class of the invertible sheaf \(f^*\Omega_S^1\) is the inverse image of the total Chern class of \(\Omega_S^1\) on \(S\)
and the cup-powers of \(c^1\) are zero:
We deduce that
\[ c^{n+1}(\Omega_X^{\prime 1})= c^n(\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1})\cdot f^*c_1(S,\Omega_S^1) +c^{n+1}(\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1}). \]
If \(S\) is of genus \(g\), then \(c_1(S,\Omega_S^1)\) is equal to \((2g-2)\) times the cohomology class of an arbitrary closed point \(s\) of \(S\), whence, for \(X_s\) non-singular,

\[ I=\int_X c^n(\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1})\cdot f^*c_1(S,\Omega_S^1) \] \[ =(2g-2)\int_X c^n(\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1})\cdot f^*\operatorname{cl}(s) \] \[ =(2g-2)\int_{X_s}c^n(X_s,\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1}\otimes\mathcal O_{X_s}). \]
Moreover, on \(X_s\), \(f^*\Omega_S^1\) is isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_{X_s}\), and \(\Omega_X^1\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}\mathcal O_{X_s}\) appears in an exact sequence
\[ 0\to f^*\Omega_S^1\otimes\mathcal O_{X_s}\to\Omega_X^1\otimes\mathcal O_{X_s}\to\Omega_{X_s}^1\to0. \]
From this, we deduce that

\[ c^n(X_s,\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1}\otimes\mathcal O_{X_s}) =c^n(X_s,\Omega_{X_s}^1)\quad\text{and} \] \[ I=(2g-2)\int_{X_s}c^n(X_s,\Omega_{X_s}^1)=(-1)^{n+1}\chi(S)\chi(X_s)\quad\text{and} \]
The canonical map
\[ f^*:f^*\Omega_S^1\longrightarrow\Omega_X^1 \]

is identified with a section \(a\) of \(\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1}\). Once systems of local coordinates on \(S\) and \(X\) have been chosen, which identifies \(\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1}\) with \(\mathcal O_X^{n+1}\), the coordinates of \(a\) are the partial derivatives \(\partial f/\partial x_i\). The section \(a\) thus van- ishes only on \(F\), and by 1.3, the multiplicity of the zero of \(a\) at \(x\in F\) is equal to the Milnor number \(\mu(X/S,x)\).

Let us recall that if a section \(a\) of a vector bundle \(G\) of rank \(d\) over a proper smooth \(k\)-scheme \(X\) purely of dimension \(d\) vanishes only at isolated points, then the trace \(\int_X c^d(G)\) is equal to the number of zeros of \(a\), counted with their multiplicities. We have here
\[ II=\int_X c^{n+1}(\Omega_X^1\otimes(f^*\Omega_S^1)^{\otimes-1}) =\sum_{x\in F}\mu(X/S,x). \]
Regrouping the terms, we find
whence 2.1 since \(\chi(X_s)=\chi(X_{\bar\eta})\).

blocks.title: Corollary 2.2.
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2.

Under the preceding hypotheses, if \(n(X/S,x)\) is the total number of vanishing cycles at \(x\) of \(X/S\) (1.8), we have

\[ \sum_{x\in F}\mu(X/S,x)=\sum_{x\in F}n(X/S,x). \]
The Leray spectral sequence for \(f\) furnishes
\[ \chi(X)=\sum(-1)^{p+q}\dim_{\mathbb Z/\ell}H^p(S,R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell) =\sum_q(-1)^q\chi(S,R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell). \]
For every constructible sheaf \(G\) of \(\mathbb Z/\ell\)-modules on \(S\), we have ([4] or SGA 5 X 7.2)
\[ \chi(S,G)=\chi(S)\cdot\dim_{\mathbb Z/\ell}(G_{\bar\eta})-\sum_{s\in S}\varepsilon_s(G), \]
where, for each \(s\in S\), if we identify \(\bar\eta\) with the algebraic closure of the field of fractions of the henselization of \(S\) at \(s\), we have
\[ \varepsilon_s(G)=\dim(G_{\bar\eta})-\dim(G_s)-\dim\operatorname{Sw}(G_{\bar\eta}). \]
For \(s\in S\), we deduce from the exact sequences XIII 2.4.6.3
\[ \cdots\ (R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar\eta}\longrightarrow(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)_s \longrightarrow\bigoplus_{x\in F(s)}\Phi^q_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)_x\longrightarrow\cdots \]
that

\[ \tag{a}\sum(-1)^q\bigl(\dim(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar\eta} -\dim(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)_s\bigr) =\sum_{x\in F(s)}\sum_q(-1)^q\dim\Phi^q_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)_x, \] \[ \tag{b}\sum(-1)^q\dim\operatorname{Sw}(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar\eta} =\sum_{x\in F(s)}\sum_q(-1)^q\dim\operatorname{Sw}\Phi^q_{\bar\eta}(\mathbb Z/\ell)_x, \] \[ \tag{c}\sum(-1)^q\varepsilon_s(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell) =\sum_{x\in F(s)}\dim\operatorname{tot}\Phi_x. \]
The Euler-Poincaré formula thus reduces to

\[ \begin{aligned} \chi(X)&=\sum(-1)^q\chi(S,R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)\\ &=\sum_q(-1)^q\left(\chi(S)\dim(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar\eta} -\sum_s\varepsilon_s(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)\right)\\ &=\chi(S)\sum_q(-1)^q\dim(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)_{\bar\eta} -\sum_s\sum_q(-1)^q\varepsilon_s(R^qf_*\mathbb Z/\ell)\\ &=\chi(S)\chi(X_{\bar\eta})-\sum_s\sum_{x\in F(s)}\dim\operatorname{tot}\Phi_x\\ &=\chi(S)\chi(X_{\bar\eta})-(-1)^n\sum_{x\in F}n(X/S,x). \end{aligned} \]
Comparing this formula with (2.1.1), we find 2.2.

blocks.title: Corollary 2.3.
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3.

Under the preceding hypotheses and with the preceding notation, if \(x_o\in F\) and if for every \(x\in F\) distinct from \(x_o\), the fiber of \(f\) passing through \(x\) presents at \(x\) an ordinary quadratic singular point, then

\[ \mu(X/S,x_o)=n(X/S,x_o). \]
For \(x\ne x_o\), we have by 1.13
and it remains to apply 2.2.

blocks.title: Theorem 2.4.
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.4.

Let \(S\) be a henselian trait of equal characteristic with perfect residue field, \(f:X\to S\) a flat morphism of finite type and \(\bar x\) a geometric point of \(X\), whose image in \(X\) is a closed point \(x\) of \(X_s\) which is an isolated point of non-smoothness of \(X/S\). We suppose that \(X\) is regular at \(x\). Then the total number (1.8) of vanishing cycles of \(X/S\) at \(\bar x\) coincides with the Milnor number \(\mu(X/S,x)\).

Let \(S'\) be the henselian trait deduced from \(S\) by extension of the residue field of \(k(s)\) to its separable closure, and let \(X'=X\times_S S'\). The assertion 2.4 for \(X/S\) is then equivalent to 2.4 for \(X'/S'\). Since \(k(s)\) is already assumed perfect, we may thus assume the residue field of \(S\) algebraically closed.

For such an \(S\), let \(S'\) be the completion of \(S\), and \(X'=X\times_S S'\). The Milnor number of \(X/S\) at \(x\) is the same for \(X/S\) and \(X'/S'\). By XIII 2.4.2 the same holds for the total number of vanishing cycles. This allows us to assume that \(S\) is moreover complete, hence isomorphic to \(k[[t]]\) (with \(k\) algebraically closed). We then have, for \(X_s\) of dimension \(n\) at \(x\):

blocks.title: Proposition 2.5.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.5.

There exist a smooth projective variety \(Y\) over \(k\), purely of dimension \(n+1\), a morphism \(f:Y\to\mathbb P_k^1\), smooth outside a finite set \(F\) of points of \(Y\), and \(y_o\in F\) such that

\(a\) if \(y\in F\), \(y\ne y_o\), then \(y\) is an ordinary quadratic singular point of the fiber of \(f\) passing through \(y\);

\(b\) we have \(f(y_o)=0\), and if \(a:S\to\mathbb P_k^1\) is the natural isomorphism between \(S\) and the completion of \(\mathbb P_k^1\) at \(0\), there exists an \(S\)-isomorphism between the henselization of \(X\) at \(x\) and the henselization of \(a^*Y\) at \(y_o\).

Let us show that 2.5 \(\Rightarrow\) 2.4. By 2.3 and 2.5 \(a\), the Milnor number of \(Y/T\) at \(y_o\) coincides with the total number of vanishing cycles. As above, by XIII 2.4.2, we have the same result for \(a^*Y/S\), hence also for \((X/S,x)\), which by 2.5 \(b\) is locally isomorphic to \((a^*Y/S,y_o)\) for the étale topology.
Let us prove 2.5. The completion of the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is isomorphic, as a \(k[[t]]\)-algebra, to a quotient
\[ \widehat{\mathcal O}_{X,x}=k[[t,x_1,\ldots,x_{n+1}]]/(f). \]

We then deduce from the implicit function theorem XV 1.1.2 (= [1] lemma 5.10) that there exists an integer \(N\) such that condition 2.5 \(b\) is satisfied as soon as \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{Y,y_o}\) is (k[[t]])-isomorphic to \(k[[t,x_1,\ldots,x_{n+1}]]/(f_1)\), with \(f_1-f\) in the \(N\)-th power of the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) of \(k[[t,x_1,\ldots,x_{n+1}]]\).

If \((t_o,t_1)\) and \((x_o,\ldots,x_{n+1})\) are the usual systems of homogeneous coordinates on \(\mathbb P^1\) and \(\mathbb P^{n+1}\), we shall take for \(Y/\mathbb P^1\) the subvariety of \(\mathbb P^1\times\mathbb P^{n+1}\) defined by an equation

homogeneous of degree \(d'\) in \((t_o,t_1)\) and homogeneous of degree \(d''\) in the \(x_i\). In more intrinsic terms, \(g\) will be a section of \(\mathcal O(d',d'')=\operatorname{pr}_1^*\mathcal O(d')\otimes\operatorname{pr}_2^*\mathcal O(d'')\).
The condition \(b\) will then be satisfied if
\[ \tag{2.5.2}g(1,t;1,x_1,\ldots,x_{n+1})\equiv f\pmod{\mathfrak m^N}. \]

Let us fix \(d'\) and \(d''\). The \(g\) of type (2.5.1) are then the (ra- tional) points of an affine space \(A\) over \(k\). Let \(u\in\mathbb P^1\times\mathbb P^{n+1}\). We shall denote by \(B(u,k)\) the affine space whose points are the \(k\)-jets of sections of \(\mathcal O(d',d'')\) at \(u\). If \(u_o=(1,0;1,0,\ldots,0)\) and if \(u\ne u_o\), then, for \(d'\) and \(d''\) large enough, the restriction map
\[ \tag{2.5.3}A\longrightarrow B(u_o,N)\times B(u,2), \]
which is always linear, is surjective. Let \(C(u)\subset B(u,2)\) be the set of 2-jets \(h\in B(u,2)\) which are the 2-jet of a germ \(\widetilde h\) which is zero or such that
\(a\) \(u\) lies on the variety \(V\) with equation \(\widetilde h=0\);
\(b\) \(u\) is neither a smooth point nor an ordinary quadratic point of the fiber passing through \(u\) of \(V/\mathbb P^1\).
One easily verifies that \(C(u)\) is closed of codimension \(n+3\) in \(B(u,2)\).

Let \(D\) be the affine subspace of \(A\) formed by the \(g\) satisfying (2.5.2). For \(u\ne u_o\), let \(D(u)\) be the subspace of \(D\) formed by the \(g\) which are zero or such that \(g(u)=0\) and that \(u\) is neither a smooth point nor an ordinary quadratic point of the fiber passing through \(u\) of the variety \(V\) with equation \(g=0\). Then, if \(d'\) and \(d''\) are large enough for (2.5.3) to be surjective, \(D(u)\) is of codimension \(n+3\) in \(D\). The union of the \(D(u)\) for \(u\ne u_o\) is thus of codimension \(\ge1=(n+3)-(n+2)\), and a general element \(g\) of \(D\) is in none of the \(D(u)\). It solves the posed problem.
